coredata.dc:title: Facile two-step synthesis of nickel nanoparticles supported on 3D porous carbon frameworks as an effective electrocatalyst for urea and methanol oxidation
coredata.dc:creator: Elsawy, Hany
Thamer, Badr M.
Sedky, Azza
El-Newehy, Mohamed H.
coredata.dc:description: The design and development of facile and environmentally-friendly methods to prepare low-cost, high-activity electrocatalysts are desirable for the development of fuel cells. In our study, a high-efficiency anodic electrocatalyst for the oxidation of urea and methanol based on nickel nanoparticles supported on three-dimensional porous carbon frameworks (Ni/3DPCFs) was designed by an innovative and simple method. This innovative method is based on the preparation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and nickel ions (Ni2+) adsorbed on its surface by microwave-assisted synthesis, followed by pyrolysis. The method is notable for simultaneously preparing Ni2+ and CQDs and not requiring a stabilizer, surfactant, or organic solvent. The results of scanning and transmission electron microscopy (SEM and TEM) revealed that the CQDs were converted to 3DPCFs by pyrolysis and nickel nanoparticles (NiNPs) were formed on the surface. The prepared electrocatalyst showed high efficiency for the oxidation of urea and methanol in alkaline media. The electrocatalytic activity of Ni/3DPCFs showed the maximum anodic current density of 102.5 and 153.8 mA/cm2 for urea and methanol oxidation, respectively. This method can provide a simple and novel approach to producing various metallic nanoparticles supported on 3DPCFs as electrocatalysts by two-step microwave-assisted synthesis and pyrolysis without using toxic reagents and surfactants.

(a) TEM image of CQDs, (b) FE-SEM image of three-dimensional porous carbon frameworks (3DPCFs), (c) FE-SEM image of Ni/3DPCFs and (d) TEM images of Ni/3DPCFs.
EDX analysis of (a) 3DPCFs and (b) Ni/3DPCFs. (c) XRD spectrum of CQDs, 3DPCFs and Ni/3DPCFs.
TGA/DTA analysis of (a) CQDs, (b) 3DPCFs, (c) Ni2+/CQDs and (d) Ni/3DPCFs.
CVs of Ni/3DPCFs in a non-Faradaic region of voltammograms at scan rates of 10–100 mV/s in 1.0 M KOH, and (d) cathodic and anodic current densities measured vs. scan rate.

Superimposed CV for (a) 3DPCFs and Ni/3DPCFs in 1 M KOH, (b) GCE, 3DPCFs and Ni/3DPCFs in 0.3 M urea/1.0 M KOH (c) GCE, 3DPCFs and Ni/3DPCFs in 3 M methanol in 1 M KOH solution (d) Ni/3DPCFs in 1 M KOH, 0.3 M urea and 3 M methanol + 1 M KOH. All CV at a scan rate of 50 mV/s.
(a, b) Effect of urea concentration and (c, d) effect of methanol concentration on the electrocatalytic activity of Ni/3DPCFs.
Nyquist plots of Ni/3DPCFs for (a) urea oxidation and (b) methanol oxidation at various potentials in 1 M KOH containing 0.3 M urea and 1.0 M methanol.

(a and c) CV measurements of Ni/3DPCFs in 0.3 M urea and 2.0 M methanol at different scan rate, and (b and d) plotting of anodic and cathodic current densities vs. the square root of scan rate for urea and methanol oxidation, respectively.
Chronoamperometric test in KOH solutions for 5000 s (a) 0.3 M urea at a potential value of 1.46 V and (b) 2.0 M of methanol at a potential value of 1.66 V vs. RHE.
Comparative electrochemical performance of various electrocatalysts for methanol and urea oxidation.

head.ce:title: Facile two-step synthesis of nickel nanoparticles supported on 3D porous carbon frameworks as an effective electrocatalyst for urea and methanol oxidation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The design and development of facile and environmentally-friendly methods to prepare low-cost, high-activity electrocatalysts are desirable for the development of fuel cells. In our study, a high-efficiency anodic electrocatalyst for the oxidation of urea and methanol based on nickel nanoparticles supported on three-dimensional porous carbon frameworks (Ni/3DPCFs) was designed by an innovative and simple method. This innovative method is based on the preparation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and nickel ions (Ni2+) adsorbed on its surface by microwave-assisted synthesis, followed by pyrolysis. The method is notable for simultaneously preparing Ni2+ and CQDs and not requiring a stabilizer, surfactant, or organic solvent. The results of scanning and transmission electron microscopy (SEM and TEM) revealed that the CQDs were converted to 3DPCFs by pyrolysis and nickel nanoparticles (NiNPs) were formed on the surface. The prepared electrocatalyst showed high efficiency for the oxidation of urea and methanol in alkaline media. The electrocatalytic activity of Ni/3DPCFs showed the maximum anodic current density of 102.5 and 153.8 mA/cm2 for urea and methanol oxidation, respectively. This method can provide a simple and novel approach to producing various metallic nanoparticles supported on 3DPCFs as electrocatalysts by two-step microwave-assisted synthesis and pyrolysis without using toxic reagents and surfactants.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Growing concerns about the energy crisis and environmental issues have prompted researchers to find alternative sources of energy in recent decades. Fuel cells are alternative sources of energy that can play a significant role in providing clean, green, and sustainable energy [1–3]. Among the fuel cells that have received wide attention from researchers are direct methanol fuel cells (DMFCs) and direct urea fuel cells (DUFCs). Methanol is a desirable fuel for fuel cells due to its high energy density and simplicity of storage, as well as the versatility of its production sources [4,5]. The main advantage of urea over other conventional fuels is that it is solid, non-toxic, non-flammable, and can be produced from low-cost sources [6]. However, the oxidation reaction of urea and methanol on the surface of the anode in fuel cells still faces some challenges such as sluggish kinetics and high overpotential [7,8]. To overcome these challenges, researchers have made great efforts to design catalysts that are able to improve the kinetics of the methanol and urea oxidation reaction and reduce the onset potentials. Catalysts of precious metals such as platinum, palladium and ruthenium, have been designed as efficient catalysts to accomplish this purpose, but their high cost and scarcity are a barrier limiting the use of DMFCs and DUFCs on a commercial scale [9–16]. Since the methanol oxidation reaction (MOR) and urea oxidation reaction (UOR) take place in an alkaline medium, the design of low-cost transition metal-based catalysts is an alternative option to precious metals because they do not corrode in the alkaline medium [17]. Nickel-based catalysts are among the most widely used catalysts for the MOR and UOR in alkaline media due to the formation of an active layer of NiOOH on the surface at high potential [18–22]. Although nickel-based catalysts have shown a comparable efficiency with noble metal-based catalysts, especially for urea oxidation, their efficiency is still below the level that allows for commercial application due to its low surface area and susceptibility to CO2 and CO poisoning [20,23]. Therefore, research is being conducted to develop new strategies to enhance electrocatalytic activity and the durability of nickel-based catalysts. Structural nanoscale dispersion of metal on the support and interaction between them are all factors that have a direct impact on electrocatalytic activity and stability [24–26]. High dispersion of nickel nanoparticles is usually done by utilizing supports with excellent high surface area and porosity. In addition to aiding in the uniform dispersion of catalysts, catalyst support enhances conductivity to increase charge transfer and shield catalyst nanoparticles from degradation, aggregation, and inactivation during electrochemical processes [27–29]. Carbon nanomaterials are one of the most important supports used for nickel based-catalysts due to their high surface area, porosity, high conductivity and strong nickel-support interaction [30–35]. For example, Abdel Hameed et al.; studied the effect of the type of carbon materials as a support on the electrocatalytic activity and durability of nickel-based catalyst towards urea oxidation in alkaline media [36]. They found that the electrocatalytic activity and charge transfer resistance of nickel-based catalyst was dependent on the type of carbon support.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are one kind of carbon nanomaterial that has been employed as a catalyst and catalyst support. CQDs are zero-dimensional (0D) material and have unique properties such as photoluminescence (PL), excellent optoelectronic properties, a large specific surface area, simple synthetic routes, facile surface functionalization and low toxicity [37]. Simple synthesis techniques like hydrothermal treatment, electrochemical oxidation, plasma treatment, laser ablation, ultrasonic synthesis and microwave irradiation can be used for synthesis of CQDs [38]. The abundance of functional groups on the surface of CQDs (e.g. –OH, –NH2, etc.) may offer more advantageous anchoring sites and active sites for perpetration electrocatalysts based on metal nanoparticles/CQDs nanocomposites. Some studies have shown CQDs can be employed as support for metal nanoparticles such as Pt [39], Pd [40] and Ni [41]. The strong interaction between metallic nanoparticles and CQDs results in enhancement of the electrocatalytic activity and stability of the metal nanoparticles and makes CQDs an active support. Furthermore, CQDs can be used to create 3D porous carbon frameworks (3DPCFs) with high specific surface area and electronic conductivity, excellent chemical stability and low environmental impact [42]. For example, Ji and his co-workers demonstrated that heat treatment of CQDs at 800 °C and under an inert gas of argon results in conversion into 3DPCFs [43]. The design of 3DPCFs as electrocatalyst supports has recently sparked considerable interest due to their high surface area and variety of pores that allow easier diffusion of reactants, as well as metal nanoparticle fixation and prevent their agglomeration [44,45]. However, there is a limited body of literature dealing with the fixation of metal nanoparticles on 3DPCFs and the study of their electrocatalytic activity for MOR and UOR in alkaline media. In this study, for the first time, nickel nanoparticles supported on three-dimensional porous carbon frameworks (Ni/3DPCFs) were prepared by a facile, novel, and efficient two-step method. First, microwave-assisted synthesis was used to prepare CQDs that were bonded to the nickel ions. In the next step, the pyrolysis process was used to reduce the nickel ions to nickel nanoparticles and the CQDs were converted to 3DPCFs under an atmosphere of nitrogen. The chemical composition, morphology and crystallinity of the Ni/3DPCFs were characterized by SEM, TEM, EDX and XRD techniques. The electrocatalytic activity and stability of Ni/3DPCFs electrocatalyst is considered for urea and methanol oxidation in 1 M KOH solution by means of cyclic voltammetry and chronoamperometry, respectively.
Citric acid, urea, methanol, nickel acetate tetrahydrate (NiAc), potassium hydroxide, isopropyl alcohol, Nafion (5%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, Darmstadt, Germany.

First, CQDs were synthesized using the method described by Qu et al. [46], with some modifications. In brief, in a 30 mL-glass vial, citric acid (1.5 g) and urea (1.5 g) were dissolved in distilled water (5 mL). The closed glass vial was then transferred to a microwave reactor (Monowave-200, Anton Paar, USA), where the reaction was carried out for 10 min at a pressure of 20–24 bar and then cooled to 55 °C, when the solution changed from a colorless liquid to a light brown liquid and then to a greenish-blue color after cooling to 25 °C. The solution was filtrated by a 0.22 μm syringe filter to separate agglomeration and high particles and was evaporated at 80 °C in a vacuum oven, and the CQDs were obtained as a powder. Finally, calcination of CQDs was done at 800 °C in a tube-furnace for 2 h at a heating rate of 5 °C/min under nitrogen gas to obtain 3DPCFs. Ni/3DPCFs were prepared by the addition of NiAc (1 g) to the citric acid/urea solution as a precursor of nickel nanoparticles (NiNPs), then following the previous steps that were used to prepare 3DPCFs as shown in Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of electrocatalytic activity of catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: First, Ni/3DPCFs catalyst (5.0 mg) was dispersed in isopropanol (400 μL) by sonication for 30 min. Next, to the dispersed Ni/3DPCFs catalyst, Nafion (20 μL) was added dropwise with continued sonication for 10 min. Then, Ni/3DPCFs catalyst ink (15 μL) was immediately deposited on the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) surface (surface area: 0.07 cm2) in three batches. The modified GCE was dried at 25 °C for 24 h followed by vacuum drying at 80 °C for 10 min. Electrochemical measurements were carried out at 25 °C with a three-electrode cell using a potentiostat (VersaSTAT3, AMETEK-USA). A modified GCE, platinum wire and Ag/AgCl (3 M KCl) was used as a working electrode, counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively. Before depositing the catalyst ink on the GCE surface, it should be cleaned with acetone and distilled water, then polished by adding one drop of diamond suspension to smooth emery paper and moving the GCE on it until it resembles a mirror, and then cleaned with acetone and distilled water again.

The synthesized CQDs and Ni2+/CQDs were characterized by the UV–vis technique. The chemical composition of CQDs and Ni/3DPCFs was examined using X-ray diffraction (XRD; Bruker, Germany) and EDX analysis. The morphology and particle size of Ni/3DPCFs were studied using field-emission scanning and transmission electron microscopy (FE-SEM & TEM; JEOL Ltd., Japan). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA; TA, USA) was used under a nitrogen atmosphere to investigate the thermal stability and define the best path of the calcination process.

UV/vis absorption spectrum of the dilute aqueous solution of CQDs and Ni2+/CQDs is displayed in Fig. 2 . UV/vis absorption spectrum of CQDs and Ni2+/CQDs exhibits absorption peaks at 332 and 330 nm and a weak shoulder around 237 and 230 nm, respectively. Two absorption peaks are attributed to the n-π* transition of outer surface functional groups and π-π* transition of conjugated aromatic domains in inner carbon core aromatic systems [47,48]. The aqueous CQDs and Ni2+/CQDs solutions were yellowish and greenish under sunlight, respectively, but they emitted a bright blue fluorescence under UV at 365 nm as shown in Fig. 2.

SEM and TEM were used to characterize the morphologies and structures of the CQDs, 3DPCFs, and Ni/3DPCFs. According to the TEM image (Fig. 3 a), the CQDs are uniform and monodisperse nanodots with an average particle size of 5.35 nm. After the pyrolysis of CQDs at 800 °C under a nitrogen atmosphere, CQDs reassembled and carbon frameworks with 3D pores and nanosheets were formed as shown in Fig. 3b. By dissolving nickel and CQDs precursors in distilled water and then performing microwave and pyrolysis processes, 3DPCFs retained their morphology with minor changes in the size of their pores and accretion layers as displayed in the SEM image in Fig. 3c. The TEM image also shows that the Ni NPs are distributed over 3DPCFs with an average particle size of 16.5 nm as shown in Fig. 3d.

The elements in the composition of 3DPCFs and Ni/3DPCFs were investigated using energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis (Fig. 4 a and b). EDX analysis confirmed that composition of 3DPCFs contained C, N and O atoms at 63.72, 26.71 and 9.57 wt%, respectively. In contrast, Ni/3DPCFs is composed of C, N, O, and Ni with a ratio of 51.88, 5.48, 2.34, and 40.30%, respectively.

Fig. 4c shows the XRD pattern for CQDs, 3DPCFs, and Ni/3DPCFs. The XRD spectrum of CQDs revealed a broad peak at 22.35° and an interlayer crystal spacing of 0.364 nm, which is larger than interlayer crystal spacing of bulk graphite (0.334 nm) [49]. This result indicates that CQDs have a turbostratic structure due to their surface being bonded with nitrogen and oxygen-containing functional groups. The pyrolysis of CQDs to 3DPCFs in a nitrogen atmosphere at 800 °C increases graphitization, as indicated by increases in peak intensity and the shift from 22.35° to 25.5°, while interlayer crystal spacing decreases to 0.348 nm as displayed in Fig. 4c. The XRD spectrum of Ni/3DPCFs revealed peaks of face-centered cubic (fcc) nickel phase at 44.42°, 51.77°, and 76.3°, as well as a broad peak of carbon phase at 25.71°. The average size of the nickel crystallite calculated by the Scherrer equation was 18.48 nm and it is close to the value obtained from the TEM image.

Fig. 5 describes the thermal decomposition of CQDs, 3DPCFs, Ni2+/CQDs and Ni/3DPCFs under a nitrogen atmosphere. According to the TGA/DTA analysis of CQDs, it was noted that the CQDs are thermally stable up to 110 °C and then the weight began to decrease gradually, with a weight loss of 5% at 140 °C due to the evaporation of physically adsorbed water. The loss of the –COOH and –NH2 functional groups as gases resulted in a sharp decrease in weight between 140 and 280 °C (weight loss in this step was 49%) [50]. After 300 °C, the carbon skeleton began to fracture, and the CQDs transform into 3DPCFs. Finally, the residual weight was 13% when the temperature reached 900 °C. On the other hand, it was observed that 3DPCFs showed high thermal stability up to 600 °C, followed by a gradual decrease in weight with increasing temperature thereafter due to the loss of their carbon structure. After 600 °C, the thermal stability of Ni/3DPCFs is observed to be lower than that of 3DPCFs, even though 3DPCFs contain more nitrogen, according to EDX analysis. This thermal behavior can be attributed to the fact that Ni/3DPCFs contain NiNPs, and thus, at temperatures above 600 °C, nickel acts as a catalyst. Consequently, recrystallization of 3DPCFs takes place and a loss of carbon mass, whereas this behavior is not observed with nickel-free 3DPCFs. The thermal decomposition of Ni2+/CQDs was also investigated to determine the best pyrolysis conditions for the preparation of Ni/3DPCFs. It can be seen that bonding nickel ions with CQDs improved thermal stability, as the gradual decrease in weight began at temperatures higher than 130 °C, compared to 110 °C for CQDs. According to the TGA/DTA analysis of Ni2+/CQDs, the weight gradually decreased to 600 °C and then stabilized, with a total weight loss of 90%. As a result, the appropriate pyrolysis temperature for preparing Ni/3DPCFs from Ni2+/CQDs was more than 600 °C under a nitrogen atmosphere.

The performance of Ni/3DPCFs as a potential catalyst for urea and methanol oxidation was evaluated by cyclic voltammetry (CV) in 1.0 KOH electrolyte at a scan rate of 50 mV/s. In general, the electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) of an electrocatalyst correlates to the number of existing active sites and their intrinsic activity [34,51]. To estimate ECSA, scan rates in the range of 10–100 mV/s at 50 mV in 1.0 M KOH were plotted vs. the difference in cathodic and anodic current density (△J) to find a double-layer capacitance (C
DL ), as shown in Fig. 6 . Ni/3DPCFs had the highest C
DL (9.15 mF/cm2), which was higher than some nickel-based electrocatalysts [34,52–55]. The ECSA value of the Ni/3DPCFs catalyst can be calculated by dividing the C
DL value by specific capacitance (C
s ) of nickel in alkaline media. The general specific capacitance of a nickel electrode is 0.04 mF/cm2 according to typical reported values [56]. The ECSA value of the Ni/3DPCFs was 228.75 cm2 based on the C
s and C
DL values, providing extra active sites for the dynamic process of methanol and urea oxidation reaction.

The Ni/3DPCFs were electrochemically activated in the 1.0 M KOH electrolyte for the 100th cycle at scan rate 50 mV/s to form an electroactive redox couple of nickel hydroxide (Ni(OH)2) and nickel oxyhydroxide (NiOOH) on the surface of the Ni NPs. Fig. 7 a displays the CV curves of 3DPCFs and Ni/3DPCFs in 1.0 M KOH. As shown in Fig. 7a, the peak of anodic current density and cathodic current density were observed at 1.36 V and 1.23 V, respectively, due to the formation of a thin layer of NiOOH by oxidation of Ni(OH)2. At potentials greater than 1.56 V, the current density is due to the oxygen evolution reaction (4OH− → O2 + H2O + 4e−). Fig. 7b and c shows the electrochemical performance of Ni/3DPCFs catalysts for urea and methanol oxidation in an alkaline solution and compare it with GCE, 3DPCFs, and Pt/C catalyst. It was observed that the Pt/C catalyst did not show any oxidation peak in the range of 1.36–1.6 V, which indicates its ineffectiveness in relation to the oxidation of urea and methanol. These results are consistent with the results obtained by Barbosa et al., where they found that the Pt/C catalyst is ineffective towards methanol oxidation [57]. The Ni/3DPCFs catalyst shows high electrocatalytic activity with a current density of 102 and 154 mA/cm2, respectively, at 1.71 V vs. RHE. In the urea oxidation reaction, the current density was decreased at a potential above 1.46 V and this was followed by the appearance of a secondary anodic peak at a potential higher than 1.56 V. This behavior is due to poisoning of the catalyst with urea oxidation intermediates followed by reactivation of its surface at a potential above 1.56 V [58]. On the other hand, it was observed that the Ni/3DPCFs were more effective in the oxidation of methanol, as a single anodic peak appeared and reached its maximum value at a potential of 1.71 V as displayed in Fig. 7c. To compare and verify the electroactivity of the prepared catalyst in the oxidation of urea and methanol in alkaline media, CV was performed in a solution of 1 M KOH and then in 1 M KOH containing 0.3 M urea and 3.0 M methanol as shown in Fig. 7d. It can be seen from the CV in 1 M KOH without urea and methanol that the anodic peak appeared at 1.3 V, which was attributed to the formation of a thin layer of NiOOH on the surface of the catalyst. Further scanning of a more positive potential, OER occurred on the prepared catalyst, as indicated by the sharp increase in the anodic current after 1.6 V. When urea was added, a high anodic current peak could be observed for urea oxidation with an onset potential very close to the onset potential of the NiOOH layer formation as displayed in the insert in Fig. 4d. This result indicates that the NiOOH layer was formed simultaneously with the reaction of urea oxidation and that the mechanism of urea oxidation depends on the formation of that layer. In contrast, it was observed that the onset potential of methanol oxidation was higher than the onset potential of NiOOH formation, which indicates that the methanol oxidation mechanism on the catalyst surface differs from the urea oxidation mechanism.

The concentration of the reactants is one of the most important factors that strongly influences the reaction rate, especially for non-zero order reactions. Fig. 8 illustrates the study of the influence of urea and methanol concentration on the electrocatalytic activity of Ni/3DPCFs and anodic peak potential. The concentration of urea was studied in a range of 0.0–0.5 M, while methanol was studied in a range of 0.0–3.0 M, at a scanning rate of 50 mV/s. The corresponding anodic peak current density and anodic peak potential as a function of urea and methanol concentration is displayed in Fig. 8 and Fig. S1. The maximum of anodic current density and anodic peak potential increases linearly with increasing urea concentration, rising from 22.13 to 39.44 mA/cm2 and from 1.44 to 1.46 V, respectively, by increasing the urea concentration from 0.1 to 0.4 M. In methanol oxidation, the maximum anodic current density is increased from 114.8 to 153.8 mA/cm2 by increasing the methanol concentration from 0.1 to 2.0 M and then becoming negligible as displayed in Fig. 8c and d and Fig. S2. The anodic peak potential value shifted to a more positive value by increasing the methanol concentration from 0.1 to 1 M, followed by a slight shift at higher concentrations. This can be attributed to an increase in the concentration of intermediates and unoxidized organic residue with increasing methanol concentration, which necessitates a higher potential [21]. Generally, the anodic peak current density with increasing urea and methanol concentrations is observed to level off at concentrations greater than 0.4 M and 2 M, respectively. This behavior can be ascribed to the presence of a greater number of electroactive sites available on the surface of Ni/3DPCFs catalyst at lower concentrations of urea or methanol, compared to the number of their reactant molecules, which speeds up the reaction kinetics. Furthermore, the reaction at low concentrations of urea or methanol is controlled by the diffusion process. In contrast, at higher concentrations, the oxidation reaction was controlled by the adsorption process, which was inhibited by the adsorption of intermediates on the electrode surface as well as a lack of hydroxy ions in the solution [59].

The Nyquist plots impedance of the Ni/3DPCFs electrode for urea and methanol oxidation at various potentials in 1 M KOH containing 0.3 M of urea and 1 M of methanol are displayed in Fig. 9 . The Nyquist plots of methanol oxidation showed semicircles in the low-frequency zone which can be observed at a potential more than 1.41 V. The Nyquist plots of urea and methanol oxidation showed a very small and depressed arc in the high-frequency zone, while a large semicircle in the low-frequency zone can be observed at a potential of more than 1.41 V. This result confirmed that the kinetics of urea and methanol electrooxidation at the surface of Ni/3DPCFs were enhanced at high potential, whereas at low potential, the oxidation of Ni/NiOOH species dominates, as demonstrated by the CV results. At an applied potential of 1.46 V for urea oxidation and 1.56 V for methanol oxidation, the charge transfer resistance (R
ct ) was 12.91 and 13.94 Ω, respectively, indicating that the kinetic of methanol oxidation was higher than that of urea oxidation.

The influence of different scan rates on the electrocatalytic activity of Ni/3DPCFs for urea and methanol oxidation in 0.3 M and 2.0 M, respectively, was evaluated in Fig. 10 . As shown, it was noted that increasing the scan rate led to increasing the peak current densities for urea and methanol oxidation as well as cathodic peak current densities. This behavior is attributed to the decrease in the diffusion layer with an increasing scan rate [60]. Furthermore, slight positive shifts in the anodic oxidation peaks of urea and methanol were observed. However, negative shifts in the cathodic oxidation peaks of urea and methanol were noted, indicating the reversibility of the Ni2+ to Ni3+ transformation [61]. Fig. 10b and d shows plots of anodic and cathodic current densities vs. the square root of the scan rate for urea and methanol oxidation. The cathodic and current densities were increased linearly with the square root of the scan rates, indicating that the urea and methanol oxidation reactions occurred on the Ni/3DPCF surfaces and were kinetic and diffusion-controlled.

The chronoamperometry test was used to investigate the stability of Ni/3DPCFs upon long periods of urea and methanol oxidation. Fig. 11 displays chronoamperograms in 0.3 M urea and 2.0 M methanol in 1.0 M KOH for 5000 s at an applied potential of 1.46 and 1.66 V vs. RHE, respectively. In the first seconds, it was observed that the current density dropped sharply, whether in the reaction of urea or methanol, due to the increase in the rate of consumption of urea or methanol molecules at the interface between the Ni/3DPCFs catalyst and the electrolyte as well as the accumulation of highly adsorbed poisoning intermediate species on active sites [62]. After the sharp decline, the current gradually reached a pseudo-steady. To further study the electrochemical durability of Ni/3DPCFs as an electrocatalyst for urea and methanol oxidation, its stability was investigated by conducting repeated CVs (Fig. S3). Even after the 50th cycle, Ni/3DPCFs catalyst showed a relatively low drop in current density for either urea oxidation or methanol oxidation.

Through the aforementioned investigations, we have proved that Ni NPs supported on a 3D porous carbon structure can be prepared by microwave-assisted synthesis and pyrolysis from non-precious precursors. The prepared electrocatalyst has good electrocatalytic activity and stability for the urea and methanol oxidation reaction in alkaline electrolyte. The immobilization of nano-sized Ni NPs on the surface of a 3D porous carbon structure allows for greater diffusion of methanol and urea molecules on the catalyst surface, resulting in an increased electrocatalytic activity. The good electrocatalytic activity of prepared Ni/3DPCFs electrocatalyst could be investigated by comparing the measured anodic peak current density of urea and methanol oxidation with previously reported results at various nickel-based electrocatalysts [25,34,63–66] as listed in Table 1 .

A novel strategy based on a microwave-assisted synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and nickel ions (Ni2+), followed by pyrolysis was reported as effective for the preparation of Ni/3DPCFs and non-precious electrocatalysts for urea and methanol oxidation. SEM and TEM images confirmed that pyrolysis converted CQDs to 3DPCFs, and NiNPs were formed on the surface. XRD confirmed the formation of a face-centered cubic (fcc) nickel phase with a crystallite size average of 13.89 nm and an amorphous carbon phase with interlayer crystal spacing of 0.348 nm. The electrocatalytic activity of Ni/3DPCFs towards urea and methanol oxidation was investigated by cyclic voltammetry in alkaline media. Ni/3DPCFs had the highest C
DL (9.15 mF/cm2), which was higher than some nickel-based electrocatalysts. The prepared electrocatalyst exhibited excellent performance for urea and methanol oxidation and the maximum anodic current density values were 102.5 and 154 mA/cm2, respectively. This study may thus represent a significant advance in the design and development of a facile, novel, and eco-friendly method to produce low-cost and effective electrocatalysts for various applications.

Hany Elsawy: Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition. Badr M. Thamer: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Azza Sedky: Investigation, Data curation, Software. Mohamed H. El-Newehy: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.
